opp.name: Hollard_2025
opp.next_step: April 2026: Projecy postponed till second half March 2: There is some interest to look into Zycus again as they going through ERP upgrade now
opp.ais_why:
• Product scope is the single SKU 'eProcurement' ‚Äî no Merlin, no ANA, no Agentic AI, no ML iAnalyze, no Merlin Intake. This is an explicit single-module traditional buy at $150K. The scope shape is the strongest possible signal that AI is not in play: a single-module eProcurement evaluation cannot be AI-led by definition.
• 10 Avoma meetings Jan-Apr 2025 show heavy historical engagement but LastActivityDate is 2025-06-02, meaning the opp has been quiet for ~11 months. The historical meeting volume confirms real engagement at one point, but the current cadence is dormant. No AI products in scope means no AI conversation grounds ‚Äî the dialogue would have been entirely eProcurement-functional.
• Medium confidence: product scope is the dominant signal (single eProcurement SKU). The opp appears stalled with no recent activity for nearly a year. Score 1 (AI Resistant) is unlikely to change without a scope expansion to include Merlin/AI modules and a re-engagement event. The combination of single-product scope + 11-month dormancy is doubly negative for AI relevance.

items.area: No transcribed buyer call in 13+ months

sources: Avoma call 2 Apr 2025 (Denver Archary, Jochen Werner)
metrics.narrative: Buyer articulated clear qualitative pain but never quantified savings or ROI. Nomonde Thabethe described manual, spreadsheet-based contract management ('currently we do contracts manually, we have got a spreadsheet where we say the contract expiring... it's very painful', 28 Mar 2025) and a fragmented procurement estate spanning insurer, life, claims and group procurement across seven African countries. Denver Archary framed the driver as sourcing efficiency, not a numeric business case ('the need for now is the eyesource... hence why the budget is at that value', 2 Apr 2025). Jochen Werner offered a free value assessment to build the case ('we do something sometimes as a value assessment, just supporting it', 2 Apr 2025) but there is no evidence a quantified model was ever produced. The move is to co-build a savings/cycle-time model procurement can present to its executives to unlock budget.

champion.narrative: Nomonde Thabethe (Strategic Sourcing Manager) was a strong champion from January to April 2025: she led discovery, drove internal alignment across legal, claims and broker teams, pushed a phased proposal to fit budget, and coordinated the commercial negotiation ('we went back to discuss the pricing... we discussed how do we make this go over the line', 2 Apr 2025). Denver Archary (Procurement Manager) acted as the co-decision driver. What developed: genuine solution preference and an internal plan. What is missing now: any engagement since 2 June 2025, so the champion has gone cold and may have been reassigned or deprioritised. Advocacy strength has decayed to weak/at-risk; the move is to re-confirm Nomonde still owns procurement tech selection or identify her successor.

sources: Avoma call 2 Apr 2025 (Denver Archary, Jochen Werner)
competition.narrative: This is a replacement deal, not an active bake-off: the buyer is displacing an unnamed incumbent procurement provider they have used 'for many years' and is running a parallel group finance/ERP transformation (Microsoft Dynamics is going end-of-life around 2027-2028). No competitor is currently preferred over Zycus. Coupa surfaced only as background (Zycus's own VP previously worked there, and Zycus disparaged it post-Thoma-Bravo acquisition); the buyer noted Coupa's Gartner standing neutrally but showed no lean. Denver did note 'there are other solutions we are within budget on the costing' (2 Apr 2025), so price-competitive alternatives exist even if unnamed. The competitive risk is displacement-by-ERP (if the new finance platform bundles procurement) plus the budget gap, not a named rival winning.

identify_pain.narrative: Confirmed operational pain: fully manual, spreadsheet-driven contract management and inefficient sourcing across a fragmented insurance group. Nomonde Thabethe stated 'we do contracts manually... it's very painful and we need to automate in order for us to be able to create efficiencies and deliver on time' (28 Mar 2025) and described audit exposure and high contract volumes (2,000-3,000 group contracts, 500+ claims panel contracts). The pain owner is group procurement (Nomonde and Denver), with claims, life and broker teams as downstream stakeholders. The pain is real but deprioritised beneath the group finance/ERP transformation, which is why procurement chose to move first on sourcing only.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics were discussed at a high level: a five-year SaaS agreement, monthly-in-advance SaaS billing and milestone-based implementation payments, a scoping workshop on signature, and an IT governance path (Arc Forum, cloud assessment, InfoSec) that the buyer expected to be lengthy. Denver referenced an internal IT contact ('Bill Cobb... he was quite impressed... but he'd have to apply the full IT governance process', 2 Apr 2025). No MSA/SOW, legal review or procurement channel was ever formalised because the deal never cleared budget approval. Current-contract expiry on the incumbent was cited as roughly 2026, which could become a forcing function if the deal reactivates.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is named in Salesforce contact roles or confirmed on any call. Denver Archary (Procurement Manager) repeatedly deferred final approval to his leadership ('I need to then do a business case and motivation and then speak to my management team to see what the appetite is', 2 Apr 2025), and Nomonde noted procurement is structurally last in line for funding ('procurement when it comes to funding of technology we always the last people to be considered', 28 Mar 2025). Budget authority sits with the finance-transformation / group leadership tied to the broader ERP replacement of Microsoft Dynamics, but that person has never been engaged, named, or put on a call. The move is to have Denver or Nomonde name and introduce the budget owner before any re-quote.

sources: Avoma call 28 Mar 2025 (Nomonde Thabethe, Denver Archary)
decision_process.narrative: The buyer described a multi-gate process: secure boss approval for iSource, socialise iContract with claims, life and broker teams, run a second demo including the claims team, then submit to IT for the 'Arc Forum' governance and cloud/InfoSec assessment, followed by contracting. Nomonde named the critical path as internal approval ('everything is dependent on the approval... which I foresee might take up to June', 2 Apr 2025) against a 30 June 2025 FY-end and a mid-December IT freeze. That process stalled at the approval gate: it never advanced past Qualified, the deadline lapsed, and the deal went dark. The near-term need is to confirm whether the ERP upgrade has restarted this process.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are stated qualitatively: an ERP-agnostic, future-proofed sourcing tool that solves the immediate pain now and can extend to contract management later, with a strong contract-lifecycle module for the insurance-specific underwriting, binder and broker contracts. Nomonde stressed AI/futureproofing ('legals... very aligned to the type of solution... because it's AI driven, and that's what they're looking for', 2 Apr 2025) and phased affordability. Denver emphasised implementation-cost transparency ('what is the driver of the implementation cost... we can't unpack it', 28 Mar 2025) and an insurance reference customer to reassure conservative internal stakeholders. Price-to-budget fit is the dominant decision criterion. These signals are all from Q1 2025 and need re-confirmation.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.
blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.full: A competitor is pushing back — No competitor is currently winning this deal; it is a replacement of an unnamed long-standing incumbent procurement provider, gated by budget and by Hollard's own finance/ERP transformation. The strongest CURRENT threat is displacement-by-ERP: if the group's Microsoft Dynamics replacement (end-of-life ~2027-2028) bundles procurement, Zycus could be squeezed out before signature. Coupa is background noise only (mentioned neutrally, not preferred; Zycus argued against it). Carried-forward incumbents from the prior record are retained but unconfirmed in current evidence.

blocks.title: What moves it forward

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 17 is driven by Verdict 'Off Track' — weak forward intent. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.win_position: Win 8 is carried by momentum 54 vs stage-expected 50; business case strength +0.30 (weight 10). Offsetting it: competitive strength -1.00 (weight 15).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 54 (roughly flat): Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 3 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, stage integrity, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 0 is anchored on the stage win (8), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 54, and dragged by risk 17. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

contributions.evidence: Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is dark. Rich historical calls (Q1 2025) show real interest, but no buyer touch in ~13 months and only a rep-written reactivation note. Treat as cooling-to-dark pending a buyer-confirmed signal.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 14 Jan 2025. Owner changed from Menachem Harari to Caroline Lacocque on 27 Feb 2026; stage moved No Decision -> Qualified on 24 Feb 2026 and close date 29 Jul 2025 -> 31 Mar 2027 on 2 Mar 2026. Last verified Salesforce activity 2 June 2025 (~13 months ago). Two transcribed buyer calls read (28 Mar and 2 Apr 2025); no buyer engagement since. Close date 31 Mar 2027 is a rear-view projection with no active path, so pace required (business case, IT Arc Forum governance, legal, MSA/SOW) is entirely unbacked.
evidence: Last verified Salesforce activity 2 June 2025 (~13 months of silence)
Buyer urgency expired: 'June 30 is our financial year end... we need to get an invoice... hence we running out of time' (Nomonde Thabethe, 28 Mar 2025) — deadline passed with no contract
Stage regressed Shortlisted -> No Decision on 6 June 2025, re-opened to Qualified on 24 Feb 2026 by new owner
Close date pushed 29 Jul 2025 -> 31 Mar 2027 on 2 Mar 2026 with no buyer milestone
Next Step (rep-written): project postponed, 'some interest to look into Zycus again as they going through ERP upgrade' — no buyer call or task confirms it
Budget gap unresolved: Zycus stated '$120,000 US dollar per year' minimum vs Hollard's R1.2M all-inclusive (Jochen Werner, 2 Apr 2025)
No Economic Buyer ever engaged; Denver Archary flagged approval sits with his management team and finance-transformation stakeholders
north_star_verdict.headline: Deal has been dark for roughly 13 months (last buyer activity 2 June 2025) after the buyer's self-imposed 30 June 2025 budget deadline passed with no contract. The stage regressed to No Decision in June 2025, was re-opened as Qualified by a new owner (Caroline Lacocque) in February 2026, and the close date was pushed to 31 March 2027 with no supporting milestone. The only recent signal is a rep-written note that the project was postponed and there is 'some interest' to revisit Zycus during an ERP upgrade, unconfirmed by any buyer call or task. The real blockers are an unbridged budget gap (Zycus $120K/year floor vs Hollard's ~R1.2M all-inclusive) and an unmapped economic buyer, not a lost competitive fight.

flags: Verify the deal is alive: the only reactivation signal is a rep-written note, so secure a buyer reply or meeting within two weeks or move it to Omitted rather than carry a phantom pipeline.

Bridge the budget gap structurally: Zycus's ~$120K/year floor cannot meet a single-module sourcing budget, so anchor every re-engagement on a five-year phased iSource-first-then-iContract ramp, not a standalone quote.
Close the power gap: no economic buyer has ever engaged and procurement is 'last to be funded', so use Denver and Nomonde to name and reach the finance-transformation/group budget owner.
best_practice_check.summary: This dark, budget-blocked, early-stage deal needs a buyer-confirmed signal of life before further investment, then a phased commercial structure that bridges the budget gap, and access to the real budget owner.

competitive_position.summary: No competitor is currently winning this deal; it is a replacement of an unnamed long-standing incumbent procurement provider, gated by budget and by Hollard's own finance/ERP transformation. The strongest CURRENT threat is displacement-by-ERP: if the group's Microsoft Dynamics replacement (end-of-life ~2027-2028) bundles procurement, Zycus could be squeezed out before signature. Coupa is background noise only (mentioned neutrally, not preferred; Zycus argued against it). Carried-forward incumbents from the prior record are retained but unconfirmed in current evidence.

• Product scope is the single SKU 'eProcurement' ‚Äî no Merlin, no ANA, no Agentic AI, no ML iAnalyze, no Merlin Intake. This is an explicit single-module traditional buy at $150K. The scope shape is the strongest possible signal that AI is not in play: a single-module eProcurement evaluation cannot be AI-led by definition.
• 10 Avoma meetings Jan-Apr 2025 show heavy historical engagement but LastActivityDate is 2025-06-02, meaning the opp has been quiet for ~11 months. The historical meeting volume confirms real engagement at one point, but the current cadence is dormant. No AI products in scope means no AI conversation grounds ‚Äî the dialogue would have been entirely eProcurement-functional.

Score 1 (AI Resistant) is unlikely to change without a scope expansion to include Merlin/AI modules and a re-engagement event. The combination of single-product scope + 11-month dormancy is doubly negative for AI relevance.
